I am on the Button/Small Blind with Ace-Jack suited (Ad Jd), a premium hand in a heads-up situation. I will raise to 300 to build the pot for value and take the initiative in the hand. This is a standard opening size in this game.

With 2♣6♠ in the big blind, this is one of the weakest starting hands in poker. Flash has raised to 300 from the button/small blind. While Flash has shown aggression throughout the session, my hand has almost no equity against his likely range and minimal win probability post-flop. Even with reasonable pot odds (2:1 to call), the fundamental weakness of 2-6 offsuit makes this an easy fold. Preserving chips for better spots is the correct long-term strategy in a 150-hand session where I'm focusing on maximizing my stack. There's no value in playing this hand out of position with such poor equity.

I'm on the button (small blind) in heads-up play against Flash. I hold 6d-Th, which is a marginal but playable hand in this position. The cost to call is only 50 more to the pot (matching the 100 big blind). In heads-up play, position is extremely valuable, and I'll maintain position post-flop. Although 6-10 offsuit is not a premium hand, the cost is minimal and I can make intelligent post-flop decisions based on how the board runs out and how Flash plays. Folding here gives up my blind too easily. I'll call to see a flop and exercise my positional advantage.
Holding Q2o in the Big Blind after the Button (Claude) limped in. Q2o is too weak to raise for value or as a bluff in this spot. Checking is the standard play to see a free flop.

I am in the Big Blind in a limped pot. The flop is Ace-high (Ad 6h 5s) and I hold Queen-high with no draws. There is no reason to lead into the limper here as I have very little equity and even less fold equity on an Ace-high board. Checking is the standard play.

I'm in position (button) in heads-up play with 6d-Th on a flop of Ad-6h-5s. I have a pair of sixes with a ten kicker - a decent holding on this relatively dry board. Flash checked, indicating weakness. In heads-up poker, when my opponent checks the flop and I have a marginal hand with position, I should bet to take control of the pot and build value. A bet of 125 (about ⅔ pot) is appropriately sized - it's enough to show strength and build the pot, but not so large as to be exploitable. This forces Flash to make a decision and allows me to gain information on his holdings while maintaining my positional advantage. If Flash has missed or has a weak hand, this bet wins the pot; if he has something, I can make better decisions on later streets.

I am in a limped pot with Queen-high on an Ace-high board. Claude bet 125 into a pot of 200. I have no pair and no draws (straight or flush). My hand has negligible equity against even a small bet, and there is no reason to float or bluff on a dry Ace-high texture when out of position. Folding is the correct play.

I am in the Small Blind with 7h 6s. While it's a marginal hand, I have positional advantage over Claude for the rest of the hand. Raising to 300 is a standard move to attempt to win the blinds or to build a pot that I can take down post-flop using my position.

With K7o in the Big Blind facing Flash's raise to 300 from the button, I'm getting favorable odds to call (2:1 on 200 to win 400). While K7 is not a strong hand, it has reasonable playability heads-up and can hit top pair or flop draws. Flash has shown consistent position aggression throughout the session, but without specific reads suggesting a light raise, calling to see a flop is more prudent than 3-betting with this marginal holding. A min-raise to 500 would appear weak and commit me further with a weak hand. Calling allows me to make postflop decisions based on the board texture while preserving chips. I'm still in an early stage of the match (hand 45/150), so chip preservation with appropriate flop play is strategically sound.

I'm in the big blind on the flop facing a checked board. My hand is K7d on a Jd Tc Kc board. I've hit top pair of Kings with a 4-flush draw and 10 outs to the nuts. Flash raised preflop (300) but is now checking this connected board, suggesting either a missed hand or a slowplay. 

With K7d, I have strong equity here - top pair plus significant drawing potential. The pot is 600. I should apply pressure and build value rather than allow Flash a free turn card. A bet of 200 (~1/3 pot) is a standard c-bet size that accomplishes multiple objectives:

1. Extracts value from weaker broadway hands Flash may have missed with (AQ, AT, A9)
2. Applies pressure to continuation range
3. Sizes appropriately for my hand strength - strong enough to bet, not overcommitting with K7

If Flash raises, I can reassess. If Flash calls, I maintain decent equity and can make further decisions on the turn. The stack depths (18700 vs 20700) allow for healthy continuation if needed.

On a flop of Jd-Tc-Kc, my 7-high (7h 6s) has virtually zero equity. This board texture heavily favors the Big Blind's defending range, which includes many broadway cards and draws. Claude's lead (donk bet) of 200 into 600 indicates he likely has a piece of the board or a strong draw. There is no reason to continue with a completely air hand on such a coordinated board.

With pocket queens in heads-up position on the button, I have a premium hand that should be played aggressively. Raising to 300 (2.5x big blind) builds the pot, applies pressure, and allows me to control the action. This sizing is consistent with my recent raises and gives good value with a strong hand in position.

I am in the Big Blind with 7c 4c. Claude, on the button, has raised to 300. Although the hand is suited, 7-4 is very weak and lacks high-card value. Playing this hand out of position against an active opponent like Claude is likely to lead to a loss in the long run. I will fold and wait for a better opportunity.

We are currently playing heads-up. While opening ranges in heads-up play are typically very wide (often 70-90%), 2-6 offsuit is one of the weakest possible starting hands. Given the current stack sizes and the lack of potential for this hand to improve significantly or navigate post-flop play effectively, folding is the most disciplined and standard play here to avoid wasting chips on a marginal hand.

Calling with Q-6o in heads-up from the button. Position is strong, pot odds are favorable at 3:1, and this hand has playability post-flop. Q-6 is marginal but acceptable for a positional call. I can leverage position on later streets regardless of flop texture.

I am in the Big Blind with 8d 4c. Claude, the Small Blind, has limped into the pot. Given that 8-4 offsuit is a very weak hand, there is no value in raising here. I will take the free flop and see if I can improve my hand without committing any additional chips.

I am in the Big Blind with 8d 4c. The flop is Qc Tc 5s, which I completely missed. Claude, the Small Blind, checked to me. Given that I have no pair and no real draw on this coordinated board, there is no value in betting. I will check back to see a free turn card and hope to improve or reach a cheap showdown.

I'm in heads-up position with top pair (Queen-Six) on a Qc Tc 5s flop. Flash checked, indicating weakness. With top pair and position, I should bet for value. The board has two clubs but my pair is strong against Flash's likely checking range. A standard continuation bet builds the pot when I'm likely ahead. I'll bet approximately 60% of the current pot (120 chips) to extract value while keeping Flash engaged.

I have 8-high on a board of Qc-Tc-5s. Claude limped pre-flop and has now bet 120 into a 200 pot. My hand has no pair, no draw, and no showdown value. There is no reason to continue in this pot with absolute air against a lead on a coordinated board.

I am on the button with a pocket pair (4d 4s). Pocket pairs are strong opening hands in short-handed play. I will raise to 300 to take control of the pot and potentially win the blinds immediately or see a flop with the initiative.

AK is a premium hand that plays strongest when building the pot. In heads-up play against Flash's button raise, a 3-bet to 800 (raising an additional 500) applies meaningful pressure, builds value with my top-tier hand, and avoids the passivity of just calling 200. This sizing punishes speculative hands while giving myself a strong play that wins either pre-flop or with range advantage post-flop.

I have a pocket pair of fours on the button. I opened for 300 and Claude 3-bet to 800 from the big blind. With effective stacks at nearly 190 big blinds, the implied odds for set-mining are very favorable. I need to call 500 more into a pot of 1100, and if I hit a set on the flop, I have a strong chance of winning a much larger pot. I will call to see the flop.

Continuation betting AK on a dry flop (4c-3s-9d) after 3-betting preflop. The board texture is favorable for aggression - low cards that likely missed Flash's calling range. With two overcards and the initiative, a 900 bet (56% pot) maintains aggression while keeping solid EV. This wins the pot immediately against many missed hands and puts me in a strong position on future streets with an improving hand.

I have flopped a set of fours on a very dry rainbow board (9-4-3). My opponent 3-bet pre-flop and has now led out with a continuation bet of 900 into a 1600 pot. This board is unlikely to have hit a typical 3-betting range unless he has an overpair (AA, KK, QQ, JJ, TT). By calling instead of raising, I disguise the strength of my hand and allow him to continue betting his overpairs or air (like AK/AQ) on the turn. Slow-playing here is effective because the board is not draw-heavy, meaning there are few 'scare cards' that could kill my action or outdraw me on the next street.

Betting 1500 on the turn with AK after Flash checks. The board (4c-3s-9d-7h) is favorable for my range and likely missed Flash's holdings. Flash's check-call line on the flop followed by a turn check indicates weakness or marginal strength. With two overcards and the initiative, betting maintains pressure while building value. This sizing (44% of pot) is appropriate for heads-up play and extracts value from weaker hands while applying pressure to marginal holdings like KJ, QJ, or mid-pocket pairs.

I have a set of fours on a very safe board (9-4-3-7). My opponent, Claude, has shown aggression since pre-flop, which suggests he likely has an overpair (AA, KK, QQ, JJ) or is continuing a bluff. By calling the turn bet of 1500, I continue to disguise the strength of my hand and encourage him to fire a third barrel on the river. Raising now might force him to fold his bluffs (like AK or AQ) while he is likely to commit more chips on the river with his value range. The board has no flush draws and the most likely straight draw (6-5) is rarely in a 3-betting range, making a call the most profitable line for value extraction while keeping his range wide.

River action with AK where the King came on the river board. I now have a made hand (pair of Kings with Ace kicker). Flash checked, indicating weakness or a hand that doesn't want further aggression. Based on the calling pattern (Flash called my flop and turn bets), Flash likely has a mid-pair, weaker Ace, or draw that bricked. A 1500 bet (23% of the 6400 pot) is a standard value bet sizing for the river in heads-up play. This extracts value from Flash's weaker holdings (mid-pairs, worse Aces, missed draws) while maintaining reasonable sizing. Flash's line of checking on the river after calling down with continuing action suggests they don't have a premium hand they'd want to build with, making this an ideal spot to value bet top pair with excellent kicker.

I have flopped a set of fours and played the hand deceptively by calling Claude's bets on the flop and turn to keep his range wide and maximize value from his overpairs and bluffs. The board (9-4-3-7-K) is very clean for my hand; the only possible straight (65) is extremely unlikely to be in Claude's 3-betting range, and the King on the river is a great card for me as it likely improves his range if he had AK or KK, or encourages him to keep value-betting an overpair like AA. Claude has bet 1500 into a 6400 pot. Raising now is mandatory to extract maximum value from his likely overpairs or newly improved hands. I will raise to 4500, a sizing that looks like it could be a missed draw or a thin value bet, hoping to induce a call or even a shove from a strong overpair.

Calling 3,000 into a 15,400 pot for approximately 5.13:1 odds. Need to win ~16% of the time to break even. My pair of Kings with Ace kicker beats Flash's most likely raising range: weaker Kings (KQ, KJ, KT, K9), strong pairs below Kings, bluffs with missed draws, and weak holdings. Flash's passive flop-turn line followed by river raise suggests medium-strength value bet rather than monster, likely a hand that improved to a King or strong pair on the river. Equity advantage strongly favors calling.

8-2 offsuit is the weakest possible starting hand and is a standard fold even in heads-up play. Despite being on the button with reasonable position, this hand has minimal equity and is not worth investing chips. With a shorter stack than Flash and 100 more hands to play, preserving chips for premium hands is the optimal long-term strategy.

In heads-up play, the button (small blind) should open a very wide range of hands, typically around 80-90%. 9-6 offsuit is within the standard opening range. I have a significant chip lead over Claude (28k vs 11k) and want to maintain pressure. Js8h is too weak to defend against button aggression when out of position with a significantly shorter stack. Flash has shown consistent aggression and has a 2.4x chip advantage. Folding preserves chips for premium hands where I can better leverage position or hand strength. This is a profitable long-term fold despite the theoretical wideness of BB defense ranges.

In heads-up play on the button with K9o, this is a solid raising hand. With my stack at 11,650 versus Flash's 28,200, I need to build chips with hands that have reasonable equity. K9o has roughly 45% equity against a typical heads-up big blind calling range, making this a +EV raise. I'll maintain an aggressive posture while building the pot.

I am in the Big Blind with Ace-Deuce offsuit. Claude (on the Button) has raised to 300. I need to call 200 more into a pot of 400, giving me 2:1 odds. A2o is a standard defending hand against a button open in a heads-up or short-handed situation. While the hand is weak and easily dominated, the pot odds and my positional disadvantage are balanced enough to warrant a call to see a flop. I have a significant stack lead over Claude, which allows me to play more pots, but I should remain cautious if an Ace hits the board as I have a poor kicker.

I have top pair with the weakest possible kicker (A2o) on a board of Ah-Jh-6c. As the caller from the big blind, the standard play is to check to the pre-flop aggressor. While I have top pair, my hand is vulnerable to better Aces and various draws. By checking, I can gauge Claude's strength. If he bets, I plan to call to see a turn, as my hand is too strong to fold to a single continuation bet but too weak to build a massive pot with given the kicker problem.

On the flop with K9o against an Ace-high board, I'm in a precarious spot. My hand has whiffed completely - no pair, no draw, just King-high with a poor kicker. Flash's flop check suggests weakness, but the Ace is a dangerous card that could easily hit his range when he decides to continue.

Given my short stack (11,400 vs Flash's 28,000), I need to be selective about pot commitment. Betting into an Ace-high flop with K9 invites disaster - Flash can easily reraise with any hand that has an Ace or better, putting me in a difficult all-in situation. Additionally, I'm not deep enough to play marginal hands aggressively.

By checking, I:
1. Keep the pot small and avoid overcommitting with a weak hand
2. Allow myself to see the turn card (which could bring a King)
3. Preserve chips for stronger situations
4. Let Flash show more strength if he has it

This is a disciplined play that recognizes the limitations of K9 on this particular board texture.

On the turn, the board pairs with the 6d. I still have top pair with a weak kicker (A2), and the action has been checked through on the flop. Claude's check on the flop could indicate a variety of things: a medium-strength hand (like a Jack or a smaller pair), a draw that he didn't want to semi-bluff, or potentially a trap with a very strong hand. Since he checked back the flop, it's less likely he has a strong Ace or a set. The 6 on the turn is generally a safe card unless he was holding a 6, which is unlikely given his pre-flop raise. I will check again to see if he wants to bet. If he checks back, I'm likely ahead and will look to value bet or check-call on the river depending on the card. If he bets, I will call as my top pair is still quite strong in a checked-through pot.

Flash has checked both the flop and turn, indicating weakness. With K9 on a paired board (6c Jh Ah 6d), I have showdown value but my hand is vulnerable to most continuing ranges. Given my short stack (11,400), taking this to showdown preserves chips while keeping my options open on the river. Betting here risks losing additional chips when Flash can easily have an Ace or Jack. Checking allows the hand to go to river where I can act last and make a more informed decision based on Flash's river action.

On a board of Ah-Jh-6c-6d-Ad, I hold Ac-2h, which gives me a Full House (Aces full of Sixes: Ac-Ah-Ad-6c-6d). The only hand that realistically beats me is AJ (Aces full of Jacks), but Claude checked both the flop and the turn, making it highly unlikely he is holding an Ace or a Jack. If he has an Ace, we will likely chop the pot (as both of us would play the same Full House regardless of the kicker, unless he specifically has AJ). If he has a Jack or a pocket pair, I have him beat. Since the action was checked through on the flop and turn, I will lead out with a value bet of 400 into the 600 pot, hoping to get a call from a Jack or a mid-pair that doesn't believe I have the Ace.

Flash checked the flop and turn on an Ace-heavy board, then made a relatively small river bet of 400 into a 1000 pot. This betting pattern is telling: if he had a premium hand (Ace, Jack, or strong pair), he likely would have bet earlier or bet larger on the river.

Given pot odds of 3.5:1, I only need approximately 22% equity to break even. My K9 has reasonable value here:
- Against Flash's apparent weakness (the check-down), he could be bluffing or have a marginal hand
- K-high is a legitimate showdown value against many hands he might continuation bet with
- Flash has demonstrated frequent bluffing/aggressive plays throughout the session
- The small bet size suggests uncertainty rather than strength

My stack is 11,400 and this 400 call leaves me with 11,000. Given Flash's aggressive tendencies and the evidence of weakness in his checking pattern, calling to potentially win the pot or catch his bluff is +EV. Even if I lose, the 400 chip investment is manageable relative to my stack size.

I am on the button (small blind) in a heads-up situation. Queen-Eight offsuit is a strong enough hand to open for a raise against the big blind. I will raise to the standard 3x blind amount to put pressure on Claude and take control of the hand.

I'm holding A6o on the big blind facing a 300 raise from Flash on the button. Analyzing the situation: I'm getting 3:1 pot odds (400 in pot to call 200 more), and A6o has approximately 35-40% equity against Flash's likely button-raising range, which has been quite wide based on hand history. While out of position postflop is not ideal, the pot odds are favorable and my ace gives showdown value. Flash has been aggressive throughout and likely has many hands I can beat. Folding here would be too tight given the favorable odds and marginal extra investment required. Raising with A6o out of position would be overplaying the hand. Calling allows me to realize my equity and play the flop based on how it develops.

Top pair (ace-high) with a strong diamond flush draw on a dry flop. Flash checked, showing weakness. I'm betting 2/3 pot (400 into 600) to take down the pot or build value with my significant equity. My hand is strong enough to bet for value, and the flush draw provides significant equity cushion if called. This sizing allows me to maintain pot control while getting value from Flash's wide range.

On a board of 5d 7d Ts, I have Queen-high with one overcard (Queen) and a backdoor straight draw. However, my specific hand has very little equity against a range that includes 7x, 5x, Tx, or flush draws. Calling with just Queen-high and no direct draw is unfavorable, and raising would be turning a hand with very little equity into a pure bluff against an opponent who has shown aggression. Given the pot odds are 3.5:1, but my actual chance of having the best hand or improving to the best hand is low, the most disciplined play is to fold and wait for a better spot.

This is a heads-up situation with J7o from the button. In heads-up play, position is crucial, and the button has a significant advantage. While J7o is not a premium hand, it's definitely playable from this position. With 110+ big blinds in my stack, I can afford to see the flop and use my positional advantage. J7 has decent playability with potential for straights, and I'll be able to control the action post-flop with position. Folding here would be too tight against an aggressive opponent. Calling 50 to see the flop with positional advantage is the correct play.

I am in the Big Blind with a pocket pair of Threes (3c 3h). Claude has limped from the Small Blind. While I could raise to take the lead, pocket Threes are quite vulnerable and perform best when hitting a set on the flop. By checking, I get to see a flop for free and keep the pot small, which is a low-variance play suitable for this hand strength.

I have flopped bottom set with 3c 3h on a board of 5h-8d-3s. This is a very strong hand, but the board is relatively dry. Since the pot is very small (200) and Claude limped pre-flop, I want to start building a pot but also allow Claude to potentially bluff or catch a piece of the board. Heads-up play on the flop with J7o against a small raise from my opponent. The board is 5h-8d-3s, which is relatively dry. My hand currently has no made hand, but I have a strong gutshot straight draw (any 4 or 6 makes me a straight - 8 outs total). In heads-up play, folding to small flop bets is too tight, especially with a draw. Flash's raise could be many things - a pair, a draw, or even a semi-bluff. Calling allows me to realize my equity on the turn if I improve, and I maintain flexibility in the hand. The call is clearly +EV given the pot odds and number of outs.

I have a set of Threes on a board of 8-5-3-4. The turn 4h adds some straight and flush draws to the board. Having led the flop and been called, I need to continue building the pot for value. My hand is still very strong, likely the best hand unless Claude has exactly 67. I will bet 300 into the 400 pot to charge draws and extract value from pairs or smaller sets.

Turn decision with J7 on 5-8-3-4 board. Flash raised 100 to 300. I have an 8-out gutshot (6s, 9s make me a straight). My equity is approximately 20-22% based on 8 clean outs. Required equity for this call is 30% (pot odds of 2.33:1). Since my equity falls short of breakeven, this is a fold. In heads-up play, marginal draws can sometimes be played, but the turn aggression and poor pot odds make this a clear mathematical fold.

I am on the button with T5s. This is a speculative hand that plays reasonably well post-flop, especially in a heads-up situation. Claude has shown some aggression but also some willingness to call. I'll raise here to 300 to take control of the pot and potentially win it pre-flop, or set up a continuation bet on favorable boards. A 3x raise is standard for me in this position.

With As6s in the BB against Flash's button raise to 300, I should call the 200. Flash has been aggressive throughout and likely has a wide raising range from the button. My ace-high hand with flush potential is solid enough to defend the blind. The pot odds are favorable at 2:1, and seeing the flop allows me to play post-flop poker where I can leverage my position. Given my smaller stack, I need to accumulate chips and this is a reasonable spot to continue.

With As6s on an Ad 5h Js flop, I have top pair but weak kicker. Flash checked to me from the button after his pre-flop raise. Given my significantly shorter stack (10.8k vs 28.6k) and out-of-position nature, checking offers better pot control. This allows me to see Flash's turn action before committing more chips. Top pair can still win but isn't strong enough to build a large pot from out of position against an aggressive opponent.

On the flop of Ad-5h-Js, I have bottom pair (fives). Claude has checked to me. While the board has an Ace and a Jack which are well within Claude's calling range from the Big Blind, checking back allows me to control the pot size with a marginal hand and potentially see a safe turn card. If I bet, I might get called by better (any Ace or Jack) or forced to fold if he raises. By checking, I keep his bluffs in and can evaluate the turn.

Checking top pair with a weak kicker (As6s on Ad 5h Js Td). Flash checked the flop after raising pre-flop, indicating weakness. Out of position with a short stack, pot control is essential here. Checking allows me to see the river while avoiding overcommitting chips with a marginal hand that's vulnerable to better aces or hands like Jack-Ten.

On the turn, I have improved to two pair (Tens and Fives). Claude has checked twice, which suggests he is either playing a marginal made hand (like an Ace or a Jack) cautiously or he has missed the board. Given that I checked the flop, my range looks somewhat capped, and a bet here is likely to get called by any Ace, Jack, or straight draws like KQ. I will bet 400 into the 600 pot to extract value from these hands and protect against potential draws.

I'm holding As6s with top pair on an Ad 5h Js Td board. While my kicker is weak, several factors support calling here: (1) Pot odds are favorable - I'm facing 400 to win 1400, requiring about 22% equity, which I likely have; (2) My top pair has showdown value and potential to improve on the river; (3) Flash's turn bet sizing (400 into 1000) is moderate and doesn't necessarily represent crushing strength - he checked the flop, so his range is still wide; (4) Short-stacked situations require playing reasonable spots, and Ace-high with a vulnerable board texture is worth seeing the river; (5) Folding would be overly passive. Given my stack constraints, calling preserves flexibility for the river while maintaining my equity in the hand. If an ace or favorable river card comes, I can evaluate my position then.

On the river with two pair (As6s on Ad 5h Js Td 6d board), I'm in a strong position. Flash's turn bet of 400 after checking the flop suggests a moderate holding - likely a pair of tens or marginal two pair rather than a slow-played premium hand. My two pair beats most of Flash's likely range. Betting 450 (roughly 1/3 of the pot) is a standard value bet that extracts chips from weaker holdings while avoiding overcommitment. In heads-up play, value betting strong hands with this sizing is mathematically sound and gets called by the hands I'm trying to win chips from.

On the river, the board is Ad-5h-Js-Td-6d. I have two pair (Tens and Fives). After checking the flop and betting the turn, Claude has now led for 450 into a 1400 pot. This 'donk' bet on the river after calling the turn is often indicative of either a made flush (since the 6d completed the diamond draw) or a thin value bet with a hand like an Ace. My hand is too strong to fold in a heads-up pot, but raising is risky as I'm only likely to be called by flushes or straights (KQ). Therefore, I will call.

Folding 3s2s from the small blind. While I'm getting 3:1 odds, this is the worst hand in poker with minimal playability and virtually no equity. As a short-stacked player in heads-up, I must preserve chips for premium hands rather than speculate with trash. Flash's aggressive tendencies and my chip disadvantage make folding the prudent choice.

In a heads-up situation, Q7o is well within the standard opening range from the Small Blind. Raising allows me to take the initiative and potentially win the pot pre-flop. I will put in a standard 3x raise to 300.

Flash raised to 300 from the button/small blind. I'm in the Big Blind with K-3 offsuit (3s-Kd), a marginal hand. I need to call 200 to win 400, giving me 2:1 odds, requiring 33% equity to break even.

Analysis:

- Getting favorable pot odds (2:1)

The best play is to call and see the flop. If I hit my King, I have a strong hand. If I miss, I can make disciplined decisions post-flop based on Flash's actions and the board texture.

**Opponent assessment:** Flash has been exceptionally aggressive throughout this entire session - consistently 3-betting, applying pressure on all streets, and showing a willingness to bluff (evidenced by many pre-flop raises that don't go to showdown). This aggressive profile means his range on the river is very wide and includes many hands weaker than top pair.

**Board texture:** The final board (3c Qc 5c 2s 8d) is relatively dry. While a flush draw was possible, the 8d on the river likely missed most draws Flash would've played. The board doesn't favor nutted hands heavily, making it favorable for value-extracting bluffs.

**Risk/reward:** The bet size of 500 relative to my 10,200 stack (4.9%) is minimal. Even accounting for variance, the 3:1 odds with a reasonable hand that likely has 30-40% equity is mathematically sound.

**River decision:** Against an aggressive opponent, top pair, and favorable pot odds, a call is justified. Flash's aggressive tendencies suggest significant bluff equity in his range, making this a +EV spot.
